common_name: Pretty Shooting Star / Western Shooting Star

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall onto the surface of a moist, prepared bed; cold stratification over winter is required to break dormancy, and seedlings will emerge in early spring.

directions: Scatter fresh seed in fall onto the surface of a moist, well-prepared bed in partial shade, pressing seed lightly into the soil without covering, as light and cold together aid germination. Winter temperatures provide the stratification needed to break dormancy, and rosettes will emerge the following spring. Keep the planting area consistently moist through the brief spring growing window, then allow the foliage to yellow and die back naturally as the plant enters its characteristic summer dormancy. Once colonies are established, Pretty Shooting Star will self-sow into surrounding areas, gradually spreading into a naturalized drift.

common_name: Jeweled Shooting Star / Midland Shooting Star

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall onto the surface of well-drained, gritty or rocky soil; natural cold stratification through winter is essential for reliable spring germination.

directions: Press fresh seed onto the surface of sharply drained, gritty soil in a partially shaded rock garden or limestone outcrop setting in fall, without covering deeply. Cold winter temperatures are required to break seed dormancy, and seedlings will begin to appear in early to mid-spring. Jeweled Shooting Star is adapted to very specific limestone habitats, so replicating those conditions with lean, alkaline, well-drained soil gives the best results. Allow the foliage to die back undisturbed in early summer as plants enter dormancy, and mark the site clearly to avoid accidental disturbance.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall onto moist, well-drained soil in a lightly shaded site; cold stratification over the Pacific Northwest winter provides the chill needed for spring germination.

directions: Sow fresh seed in early fall directly onto prepared soil in a lightly shaded, well-drained garden bed or open woodland edge, pressing seed into the surface without covering. The corms require a cold, moist winter period to germinate successfully, and seedlings typically emerge among the first wildflowers of the Pacific Northwest spring. Provide consistent moisture through the brief spring growing season, then withhold water as the foliage begins to yellow after flowering, mimicking the plant's natural summer-dry habitat. Once established, Henderson's Shooting Star will form expanding colonies and self-sow reliably under favorable conditions.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall into rich, consistently moist woodland soil in a deeply shaded site; double dormancy means germination may be delayed across two winters, so patience and an undisturbed site are essential.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall into deeply amended, humus-rich soil in a cool, deeply shaded woodland position, pressing seed just below the surface and covering with a thin layer of leaf mold. Paris japonica exhibits double dormancy, meaning the root may emerge the first winter while the shoot does not appear until the second spring, so do not disturb the planting site for at least two full years. Once seedlings finally emerge, grow them on in consistent moisture and shade, protecting them from slug damage which can devastate the slow-developing shoots. Expect many years of patient cultivation before plants reach flowering size, but the extraordinary rarity and botanical significance of this record-genome species makes it a uniquely rewarding long-term project.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in mild-winter climates for winter-to-spring bloom; in colder zones, sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.

directions: Sow seed directly into a sunny, well-drained bed in fall in zones 8-10, pressing seed about one-quarter inch into the soil and firming gently; in colder zones sow in early spring as soon as the ground can be worked. Soil temperatures between 55 and 68 degrees Fahrenheit produce the fastest and most uniform germination, so avoid sowing into cold, wet ground that could cause rot. Thin seedlings to 10-14 inches apart once they are a few inches tall, as crowding reduces air circulation and can encourage mildew. The vivid, sky-blue flowers bloom prolifically through the cool season and will continue until the onset of summer heat, after which plants can be pulled and replaced with warm-season selections.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into lean, well-drained soil in a sunny spot; cold stratification over winter improves germination, and seedlings emerge reliably in spring.

directions: Sow seed directly into a sunny, lean, and sharply drained site in fall, covering lightly with no more than one-quarter inch of soil and firming the surface. Common Bugloss thrives on neglect in poor, dry ground and will underperform in rich or overly moist soils, so resist the temptation to amend the bed with compost. Space plants generously to allow air circulation around the hairy stems and to accommodate the plant's tendency to self-sow freely; remove spent flower heads if naturalization is not desired. The vivid blue, coiled flower clusters emerge in early summer and are exceptionally attractive to bumblebees, making this plant a high-value addition to dry pollinator gardens.
